input.prompt:
Help me migrate my .NET 10 console app to .NET 11. The app:
- Creates GZipStream to compress data, then checks if the output is empty (zero bytes) to detect "no data" scenarios
- Reads TAR archives that were hand-crafted by a legacy tool (some may have invalid checksums)
- Uses ZipArchive.CreateAsync for large ZIP files and relies on lazy loading behavior
- Uses MemoryStream with very large allocations close to the maximum capacity
What breaks when migrating to .NET 11?

Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/compatibility/breaking-changes:
Use this reference section to find breaking changes that might apply to you if you're upgrading your app to a newer version of .NET.

If you're looking for breaking changes from .NET Framework to .NET, see [Breaking changes for migration from .NET Framework to .NET (Core)](fx-core).

## GitHub issues and announcements

You can also view individual issues that detail the breaking changes introduced in .NET in the following GitHub repositories:

*   For .NET Core and .NET 5+, the [dotnet/docs](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues?q=is%3Aissue+label%3Abreaking-change) repository.
*   For ASP.NET Core, the [aspnet/Announcements](https://github.com/aspnet/Announcements/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3A%22Breaking+change%22+label%3A3.0.0) repository.
*   For Entity Framework Core, the [dotnet/efcore](https://github.com/dotnet/efcore/issues?q=is%3Aopen+is%3Aissue+label%3Abreaking-change) repository.

## See also

*   [API removal in .NET](api-removal)
*   [.NET runtime compatibility](../versions/#net-runtime-compatibility)
*   [Migrate from .NET Framework to .NET Core](../porting/)
*   [Breaking changes for migration from .NET Framework to .NET Core](fx-core)

data.content: Now let me find the GZipStream and TarReader breaking changes:

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49393:
### Description

Deflate and GZip compression formats include delimiters for start and end of compressed data. The right way to compress empty payload with these formats includes only those delimiters.

### Version

.NET 11 Preview 1

### Previous behavior

`DeflateStream` and `GZipStream` would not produce any output if no data was written.

### New behavior

`DeflateStream` and `GZipStream` always write headers and footers to the output.

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Some tools do not correctly react to empty content and always expect the compressed content to contain at least the appropriate header and footer.

### Recommended action

If previous behavior is desired, we recommend special casing empty content to not try to compress it via `DeflateStream` or `GZipStream`

### Feature area

Core .NET libraries

### Affected APIs

`DeflateStream`  
`GZipStream`

* * *

[Associated WorkItem - 543462](https://dev.azure.com/msft-skilling/Content/_workitems/edit/543462)

Contents of https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49393:
### Description

Deflate and GZip compression formats include delimiters for start and end of compressed data. The right way to compress empty payload with these formats includes only those delimiters.

### Version

.NET 11 Preview 1

### Previous behavior

`DeflateStream` and `GZipStream` would not produce any output if no data was written.

### New behavior

`DeflateStream` and `GZipStream` always write headers and footers to the output.

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Some tools do not correctly react to empty content and always expect the compressed content to contain at least the appropriate header and footer.

### Recommended action

If previous behavior is desired, we recommend special casing empty content to not try to compress it via `DeflateStream` or `GZipStream`

### Feature area

Core .NET libraries

`DeflateStream`  
`GZipStream`

* * *

data.content:
Here are the four .NET 11 breaking changes that will affect your app:

---

### 1. GZipStream no longer produces zero bytes for empty input
**(.NET 11 Preview 1 — [dotnet/docs#49393](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49393))**

`GZipStream`/`DeflateStream` now **always** write headers and footers, even when no data is written. Your "zero bytes = no data" check will stop working because empty input now produces a non-zero output (the GZip header+footer).

**Fix:** Check for empty data *before* compressing, rather than checking output length.

---

### 2. TarReader now validates checksums
**(.NET 11 Preview 1 — [dotnet/docs#49394](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49394))**

`TarReader.GetNextEntry()` now throws `InvalidDataException` on invalid checksums. Your hand-crafted legacy TAR files with bad checksums will fail at read time.

**Fix:** Wrap reads in a `try/catch (InvalidDataException)`, or fix the checksums in the legacy tool's output.

---

### 3. ZipArchive.CreateAsync eagerly loads entries
**(.NET 11 Preview 1 — [dotnet/docs#50254](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/50254))**

`ZipArchive.CreateAsync` now reads the central directory **eagerly** (asynchronously) during the `CreateAsync` call. Accessing `.Entries` no longer triggers lazy I/O. If your code relied on deferred loading (e.g., catching exceptions on `.Entries` access, or expecting no I/O until first enumeration), that logic moves to the `CreateAsync` call. Malformed archives will throw `InvalidDataException` from `CreateAsync` instead of from `.Entries`.

**Fix:** Move exception handling to the `await CreateAsync(...)` call.

---

### 4. MemoryStream max capacity reduced; exception type changed
**(.NET 11 Preview 1 — [dotnet/docs#49545](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49545))**

Max capacity is now `0x7FFFFFC7` (`Array.MaxLength`) instead of `int.MaxValue` (`0x7FFFFFFF`). Allocations in the gap (56 bytes at the top) will fail. Additionally, the exception changed from `OutOfMemoryException` → `ArgumentOutOfRangeException`.

**Fix:** Cap lengths to `Array.MaxLength` and catch `ArgumentOutOfRangeException` in addition to (or instead of) `OutOfMemoryException`.
data.reasoningText:
Now I have all the relevant breaking changes. Let me summarize the four that affect the user's scenarios:

1. **GZipStream** (issue #49393): `DeflateStream` and `GZipStream` now always write headers and footers even when no data is written. So checking for zero bytes to detect "no data" will break — the output will now contain header/footer bytes even for empty input.

2. **TarReader** (issue #49394): `TarReader` now validates checksums. Hand-crafted TAR archives with invalid checksums will throw `InvalidDataException`.

3. **ZipArchive.CreateAsync** (issue #50254): Entries are now eagerly loaded during `CreateAsync` instead of lazily when accessing `.Entries`. Exceptions from malformed central directories will be thrown during `CreateAsync` rather than when accessing `.Entries`.
   
4. **MemoryStream** (issue #49545): The maximum capacity has been reduced from `int.MaxValue` to `Array.MaxLength`, and attempting to exceed it now throws `ArgumentOutOfRangeException` instead of `OutOfMemoryException`.
